answer: The maqta where Zauq names himself in connection to the Mughal court of Bahadur Shah is in chapter 22 of Diwan-E-Zauq (work slug: sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798). The closing couplet reads (transliteration): 'Un dinon garche Dakan mein hai badi qadr-e-sukhan / Kaun jaaye Zauq par Dilli ki galiyan chhod kar' — translated as: 'Though poets find their worth is great in Deccan's court today, / Who, Zauq, would leave the lanes of Delhi, and ever go that way?' This is the canonical maqta where Zauq names himself and expresses loyalty to Delhi — the seat of Bahadur Shah Zafar's Mughal court — over the competing Deccan courts. Bahadur Shah is not named by name in the verse; the connection is through Delhi as the site of his court. No maqta in the corpus names Bahadur Shah explicitly by name or title. Chapter 13 also contains a maqta referencing Delhi ('Tha Zauq pehle Dehli mein Punjab ka sa husn / Par ab woh paani kehte hain Multan beh gaya' — 'Once Delhi had the beauty of Punjab, Zauq / But now they say that water has flowed to Multan'), lamenting the city's decline, but chapter 22's maqta is the one historically associated with Zauq's connection to Bahadur Shah's court.
